questions.stem: An agent maintains several stores of information. Which of these functions as SHORT-TERM memory?
options: A knowledge graph built from prior completed tasks
The session's conversation history included in the current prompt context
Embeddings of past sessions stored in a vector database
A list of past agent mistakes persisted to disk
questions.explanation: Short-term memory is in-context information currently in the prompt window, such as session history. The other options all describe external, persistent storage, which is long-term memory.

questions.stem: An LLM cannot answer questions about your company's internal, unpublished API. Giving the agent access to the internal documentation addresses which motivation for agent memory?
options: Learning from past trials to avoid repeating mistakes
Maintaining consistency of agent behavior over time
Providing proprietary data the LLM could not have learned during training
Reducing the latency of LLM responses
questions.explanation: Proprietary or very recent information is unknown to the LLM, so the agent must supply it from external memory. Learning and consistency are other memory motivations but do not describe access to unpublished data.

questions.stem: A household robot fails to open the broken front door and notes it must use the side door. For the robot to head straight for the side door in a NEW session next week, where must that fact live?
options: In long-term memory persisted in external storage
In the current session's in-context history
In the LLM's pre-training weights
In the sampling temperature configuration
questions.explanation: Short-term, in-context memory is lost when the session ends; only long-term memory persisted externally survives across runs, letting the agent learn from past trials. Pre-training cannot contain this fact and temperature has nothing to do with recall.

questions.stem: In a pipeline where Agent 1 finds a vulnerability, Agent 2 verifies it, and Agent 3 fixes it, what is the main role of shared external memory?
options: It guarantees that the agents will not hallucinate
It replaces the need for any coordinating agent
It forces all agents to use the same underlying LLM
It lets each agent read the results written by prior agents without sharing one context window
questions.explanation: Shared external memory is the coordination layer through which each agent reads what earlier agents produced, without cramming all history into a single shared context window. It does not eliminate hallucination or coordination.

questions.stem: Under the working/episodic/semantic/procedural classification of agent memory, coding conventions, workflows, tool availability, and planning heuristics — often defined per project — belong to which type?
options: Episodic memory
Working memory
Procedural memory
Semantic memory
questions.explanation: Procedural memory holds behavioral policies and skills: tool availability, coding conventions, workflows, and planning heuristics. Episodic stores past experiences, semantic stores world knowledge, and working memory holds currently active information.

questions.stem: Which description matches WORKING memory in the cognitive classification of agent memory?
options: Small, fast, volatile storage of currently active information such as perceptual inputs
Compressed, persistent storage of the agent's knowledge about the world
A record of experience gathered from earlier decision cycles
Behavioral policies and skills maintained per project
questions.explanation: Working memory maintains active, readily available information like perceptual inputs and generated reasoning; it is small, fast, and volatile. The other options describe semantic, episodic, and procedural memory respectively.

questions.stem: A coding agent stores two notes: (1) "In run #14, npm install failed behind the proxy, so I switched to the internal mirror" and (2) "Installs behind the corporate proxy generally require the internal mirror." How do these map to memory types?
options: (1) is semantic knowledge; (2) is episodic experience
(1) is episodic experience; (2) is semantic knowledge
Both are episodic, since both were derived from experience
(1) is working context; (2) is procedural memory
questions.explanation: Episodic memory records what happened in a specific prior run; semantic memory stores abstractions that hold across cases. Note (1) describes one concrete episode while note (2) is a generalization, even though it originated from experience.

questions.stem: In the three-way division of agent memory (working context, episodic experience, semantic knowledge), EPISODIC experience is best described as:
options: Domain facts and general heuristics that hold across many cases
Open files, temporary variables, and partial plans of the current task
Behavioral skills such as available tools and workflows
A record of prior runs: decision points, tool calls, failures, and outcomes
questions.explanation: Episodic experience records what happened in prior runs — decisions, tool calls, failures, outcomes, and reflections — and serves as concrete precedent. Cross-case abstractions are semantic knowledge; the live task state is working context.

questions.stem: In the LangChain conversational-memory example shown in class, what is the purpose of the thread_id?
options: It lets different agents or conversations keep separate stored histories
It caps the number of tokens stored for each conversation
It selects which LLM will process the request
It encrypts the stored conversation history
questions.explanation: Each thread_id identifies a distinct history store, so different agents or conversations can maintain their own histories independently. It has nothing to do with token limits, model selection, or encryption.

questions.stem: Your agent's chat is nearing the context-window limit. Which memory-management strategy BEST preserves key information from early in the conversation while staying within the limit?
options: Keep only the most recent k interactions
Drop all history and answer from the latest user message alone
Summarize older interactions into a compact form kept in context
Keep the full history and truncate from the beginning when it overflows
questions.explanation: Summarization compresses older exchanges while retaining their key facts. Keeping only recent turns or truncating from the start discards early context entirely, and dropping all history breaks conversational continuity.

questions.stem: With history trimming (a sliding window), an agent limits the growth of conversational memory by:
options: Summarizing every message into a single paragraph
Keeping only the most recent k interactions (or last x tokens) and dropping the rest
Moving all older messages into a vector database
Compressing messages into knowledge-graph triples
questions.explanation: History trimming keeps a sliding window of the last k interactions or last x tokens and discards everything older. It is distinct from summarization, which condenses old content instead of dropping it.

questions.stem: In the lecture's chat-summarization program, maybe_summarize triggers once MAX_TURNS_BEFORE_SUMMARY messages accumulate. What happens to the most recent message?
options: It is summarized together with all the older messages
It is discarded once the summary has been written
It is moved into archival storage on disk
It is kept in full in state.messages and excluded from the summary
questions.explanation: The program summarizes messages[:-1] and keeps the current (last) message verbatim as the new contents of state.messages, so the freshest turn stays available in full detail rather than being compressed.

questions.stem: The chat-summarization example defines two LLMs — one to process user prompts and one to summarize the message history. What flexibility does this design provide?
options: Summarization can be delegated to a cheaper or faster model than the main chat model
The two models can share a single context window between them
It doubles the context window available to the chat model
It guarantees that summaries never lose information
questions.explanation: Separating the summarizer LLM lets you choose a different (often cheaper or faster) model for the simpler summarization task, or reuse the same one. It does not enlarge any context window or make summaries lossless.

questions.stem: According to the lecture, the value of summarizing memory such as a long chat history is that it:
options: Offloads memory to an external data store
Guarantees that no information is ever lost
Compresses the size of memory and focuses it on the key points
Makes retrieval fully deterministic
questions.explanation: Summary memory compresses the history and concentrates it on the key points. It does not offload to external stores, and by its nature it can lose information rather than guarantee preservation.

questions.stem: After several rounds of re-summarization, an agent's summary no longer mentions weather facts from the early turns. Which revision, shown in the lecture, addresses this information loss?
options: Lower the summarizer LLM's temperature so summaries are more stable
Compress Human/AI message pairs and append each pair summary to the existing summary
Re-summarize the entire history more frequently so less is lost per pass
Give the summarizer a larger context window so it sees all the turns
questions.explanation: The fix keeps the existing summary intact and appends new pair-wise summaries of recent Human/AI exchanges, so earlier content is never re-summarized away. Repeatedly regenerating one global summary is exactly what dropped the weather facts.

questions.stem: The lecture compares memory summarization in agents to which process in human memory?
options: Hippocampus-to-cortex consolidation, as in sleep and replay
Short-term sensory buffering in the retina
Muscle memory formed by physical repetition
Selective attention during perception
questions.explanation: The lecture likens summarizing agent memory to human memory consolidation from the hippocampus to the cortex, as studied in sleep/replay neuroscience — both compress experience into durable key points.

questions.stem: What distinguishes STRUCTURED memory from simply storing raw conversation text?
options: Information is stored only as embeddings for semantic search
Only summaries are stored, never the underlying facts
Facts must be stored in a proprietary database engine
Facts are organized in a data structure (e.g., a list, tree, or graph) for direct retrieval
questions.explanation: Structured memory arranges facts in a data structure such as a list, tree, or graph so the relevant fact can be retrieved directly rather than fished out of free text. Embedding-based storage is the separate vector-database approach.

questions.stem: Which statement correctly describes a knowledge graph used as agent memory?
options: Edges store the entities and nodes store the relationships
Relationships are left implicit and inferred only at query time
Entities are labeled nodes and relationships are explicit labeled edges, possibly several between two entities
Each pair of entities may be linked by at most one relationship
questions.explanation: In a knowledge graph, nodes represent entities and labeled edges make relationships explicit; two entities can share multiple relationships (e.g., 'born in' and 'worked in'). Relationships are given explicitly, not inferred.

questions.stem: Compared with keeping agent memory as plain conversation text, a key advantage of a knowledge graph is that it:
options: Always occupies less storage space than the text it came from
Makes entity relationships explicit and supports precise graph-based queries
Retrieves facts faster than any vector database
Removes any need for embeddings in the system
questions.explanation: Knowledge graphs capture entities and their relationships explicitly, enabling precise structured and pattern-based queries that plain text cannot support. Claims of universally smaller storage or faster-than-vector retrieval are not guaranteed.

questions.stem: In the lecture's NetworkX example, how is the knowledge graph populated from source documents?
options: The LLM is prompted to extract (subject, relation, object) triples from each text
A parser extracts noun phrases and links every co-occurring pair
Documents are embedded and nearest neighbors become edges
The user manually enters nodes and edges through a UI
questions.explanation: The pipeline prompts the LLM to return factual relationships as JSON triples of the form (subject, relation, object); the triples are then added as labeled edges to a NetworkX directed graph.

questions.stem: The triple-extraction prompt insists on short, consistent entity names ('Paris', not 'The city of Paris'). Why does this matter for the resulting knowledge graph?
options: It reduces the token cost of the extraction call
It prevents the LLM from hallucinating relations
It makes the graph render more legibly in matplotlib
Mentions of one entity merge into a single node, keeping its facts connected
questions.explanation: If the same entity appears under different surface forms, it becomes multiple disconnected nodes and its facts cannot be traversed together. Consistent naming ensures all facts about an entity attach to one node.

questions.stem: A team answers questions over its knowledge graph by dumping every triple into the LLM prompt alongside the question. The KG grows to millions of edges. What is the primary problem with this approach?
options: Triples cannot be serialized as plain text
The LLM will refuse to accept graph-shaped input
It does not scale — the full set of triples no longer fits in the context window
NetworkX cannot store that many edges in memory
questions.explanation: Supplying all triples in the prompt is limited by the context window and stops working as the graph grows. Production systems store the KG in a graph database like Neo4j and query it with a language like Cypher.

questions.stem: Per the lecture's comparison, which is a correct contrast between answering KG queries with an LLM (given all the triples) versus with a graph query language like Cypher?
options: The LLM is deterministic, while Cypher can vary between runs
The LLM may hallucinate or miss paths, while Cypher returns exactly what is in the graph
Cypher is limited by the context window, while the LLM scales to millions of nodes
The LLM handles arbitrary-depth multi-hop queries, while Cypher is fragile beyond 2-3 hops
questions.explanation: The LLM approach can hallucinate or miss paths, gives non-deterministic answers, is context-window limited, and is fragile beyond 2-3 hops; Cypher is deterministic, exact, scales to millions of nodes, and handles arbitrary depth. Options a, c, and d invert these properties.

questions.stem: In the LLM-plus-KG query pipeline from the software-repository QA paper, what is the correct flow for answering a natural-language question Q?
options: The LLM turns Q into a formal query K; the KG engine executes K to get result R; the LLM translates R into a natural-language answer
The KG engine parses Q directly; the LLM verifies the result; the engine phrases the final answer
The LLM answers Q from its own weights; the KG is consulted only to double-check the facts
All triples are sent along with Q to the LLM, which traverses the graph within its context
questions.explanation: The LLM is used twice: first to translate the natural-language question into a formal KG query, and after execution to translate the formal result back into a natural-language answer. The KG engine, not the LLM, executes the query.

questions.stem: The paper 'Synergizing LLMs and Knowledge Graphs' builds a knowledge graph over GitHub projects. What did it aim to support, and what was the outcome?
options: Generating new code from graph patterns, with mixed results
Compressing entire repositories so they fit into the context window
Detecting license violations across repositories automatically
Answering repository questions (e.g., which developers fixed the most bugs) more accurately than baseline methods
questions.explanation: The paper builds a KG of GitHub project data to answer questions like 'what is the latest commit' or 'which developers had the most unfixed bugs', and shows the KG approach produces more accurate answers than two baseline methods.

questions.stem: MemGPT introduced 'virtual context management' for LLMs. What does this system actually do?
options: It physically enlarges the LLM's context window to hold several times more data
It manages an external memory hierarchy plus the context-window content, enabling dynamic context construction
It allocates additional disk space automatically whenever memory fills up
It fine-tunes the LLM on past conversations so they need not be stored
questions.explanation: Modeled on OS virtual memory, MemGPT manages external memory and what occupies the fixed context window, paging information in and out. It does not enlarge the window itself, add disk, or fine-tune the model.

questions.stem: In a MemGPT-style system, recall storage reaches 90% capacity mid-session. According to the design, what happens next?
options: MemGPT silently deletes the oldest half of recall storage without informing the LLM
The session terminates until the user manually clears memory
The LLM is alerted about the memory event and can invoke MemGPT functions, e.g., to compress and archive content
The context window is temporarily expanded to absorb the overflow
questions.explanation: MemGPT surfaces memory events (like 'recall storage is at 90% capacity') to the LLM, which can then call MemGPT-provided functions to compress, archive, or search memory; MemGPT itself only steps in when necessary.

questions.stem: In the MemGPT memory hierarchy, where do prior sessions and recent-but-inactive information reside?
options: In recall storage, part of external memory
In the read-only system instructions
In the working context inside the context window
In the FIFO queue's rolling message history
questions.explanation: Recall storage (external memory) holds prior sessions and recent but inactive information; archival storage holds documents, facts, and embeddings. The working context and FIFO queue live inside the context window itself.

questions.stem: You diagnose a misbehaving agent and find it retrieves the wrong memories for its tasks. Which fix targets the actual problem?
options: Add more data to archival memory
Swap in a more powerful base LLM
Reduce the size of the context window
Improve the scoring function that ranks which memories to fetch
questions.explanation: Wrong memories being fetched is a retrieval-ranking problem, so the scoring function (e.g., relevance, recency, importance) is what needs improving. Adding more data or a stronger LLM does not fix poor memory selection.

questions.stem: In the Generative Agents ('Interactive Simulacra') paper, memories are ranked for retrieval using which three factors?
options: Length, position, and novelty
Relevance, recency, and importance
Accuracy, brevity, and diversity
Frequency, sentiment, and source
questions.explanation: Memories are scored by relevance to the current situation, recency of access, and importance (how poignant the agent judges the memory to be); the top-ranked memories are then supplied to the LLM.

questions.stem: Asked who Klaus would spend an hour with, an agent using only raw observations picks whoever he met most often. With reflection, it instead suggests Maria, who shares his research passion. What does reflection add?
options: A larger retrieval budget so more raw memories fit into the context
A recency boost that favors the latest interactions
Periodic higher-level insights generalized from many memories, retrievable like other memories
A rule engine that overrides the LLM's answer when it disagrees
questions.explanation: Reflections are more abstract thoughts generated periodically by generalizing over related memory observations; stored alongside regular memories, they let the agent answer questions that raw observation counts (like meeting frequency) cannot.

questions.stem: Context engineering is best defined as:
options: Fine-tuning a model so that its context window becomes larger
Designing and curating everything the model sees — prompt, history, retrieved documents, tool results — within the context window
Compressing embedding vectors to shrink a vector database
Replacing long-term memory with in-context learning
questions.explanation: Context engineering is the practice of designing, structuring, and optimizing the entire input the LLM receives — instructions, history, retrieved documents, and structured data — so it produces better outputs. It changes the input, not the model.

questions.stem: A teammate says: 'We already do prompt engineering, so context engineering is the same thing.' What is the accurate correction?
options: Context engineering is just prompt engineering applied to system prompts
Prompt engineering is for chatbots; context engineering applies only to RAG pipelines
Context engineering is a narrower subfield that covers only few-shot examples
Prompt engineering crafts the instruction itself; context engineering curates the whole input environment the model sees
questions.explanation: Prompt engineering is about wording the instruction — the question, format, and role. Context engineering is broader: deciding which documents to retrieve, which history to keep, summarize, or discard, and how it all fits in the window.

questions.stem: Two advisors answer 'Should I take CS 301?' using the same system prompt, but one also retrieves the course catalog, the student's record, peer reviews, and a conversation summary. Why is the second answer better?
options: The curated context supplies what the model needs to know; the prompt only told it how to behave
The retrieved text raises the model's effective parameter count
Longer inputs always yield more accurate outputs
The retrieval step fine-tunes the model on the student's data
questions.explanation: With prompt engineering alone the LLM can only answer generically; context engineering retrieves the specific facts (prerequisites, grades, reviews) the model needs. Longer input per se is not the point — relevant, high-signal content is.

questions.stem: The lecture contrasts two context-assembly strategies: supplying minimal filtered context versus supplying rich but well-structured context. Which statement reflects the lecture's view?
options: Minimal context is strictly better because tokens cost money
Rich context is strictly better because more information never hurts
It is a spectrum with trade-offs (missing context vs. token expense); Claude Code leans toward rich, structured context
Modern agents avoid the choice by fine-tuning on the codebase instead
questions.explanation: The two strategies trade off possibly missing context against token expense, and the lecture stresses this is a spectrum, not a binary choice. Claude Code follows the rich-but-structured approach, relying on a large context window.

questions.stem: A long Claude Code session triggers compaction. Which parts of the conversation get summarized, and which survive intact?
options: The earliest turns are summarized first, since they are the oldest
The most recent turns are summarized, since they are freshest in the model's mind
All turns are summarized equally to keep the history uniform
The middle turns are summarized; early problem-setup turns and recent active-work turns stay intact
questions.explanation: Compaction targets the middle of the conversation: early turns that set up the problem and recent turns with active work are preserved, while the middle is condensed to decisions, changes, and key facts.

questions.stem: In a Claude Code coding session, the context fills up unusually fast. Based on the lecture, what is the most likely dominant consumer of context space?
options: The static system prompt, which is re-sent uncached on every turn
Inline tool results, such as full file contents from reads and complete bash outputs
The user's typed messages, which accumulate quickly
The tool definitions, which are reloaded after every call
questions.explanation: Tool results live inline in the conversation history — a file read returns the full contents and a command returns all its output — making them the biggest space consumers and prime candidates for compaction. The system prompt is static and cacheable.

questions.stem: While Claude Code works, the user edits a file outside the session. How does the harness inform the LLM of the change without disrupting the conversation flow?
options: It restarts the session so the LLM re-reads all files from scratch
It appends a brand-new assistant turn describing the change
It injects a <system-reminder> tag that rides along inside an existing user message or tool result
It retroactively edits the original tool result to match the new file
questions.explanation: Runtime injections are delivered as <system-reminder> tags attached to existing messages or tool results rather than as separate turns, getting information (file changed, date changed, linter output) into context without disrupting the flow.

questions.stem: Under Claude Code's auto-memory guidelines, which of the following should NOT be saved as a memory?
options: Code architecture patterns that are derivable from reading the code
User preferences and expertise, e.g., 'senior engineer, new to React'
Confirmed feedback such as 'don't mock the DB'
Project facts such as 'merge freeze starts May 5'
questions.explanation: Auto memory skips anything derivable from the environment — code patterns and architecture (readable from code), git history, debugging fixes already in the code, and anything already in CLAUDE.md. User, feedback, and project information are the memory types it does save.

questions.stem: Claude Code delegates an expensive codebase exploration to a subagent, which runs in its own context window and returns only a compact summary. What is the key trade-off of this strategy?
options: The subagent shares the parent's context window, so nothing is actually saved
Subagents cannot use tools, which limits what they can explore
The parent's own history is erased when the subagent returns
If the subagent missed something or summarized poorly, that information is lost to the parent
questions.explanation: Subagent isolation keeps the main context clean, but the parent must trust the returned summary — the same information-loss risk seen with LLM-based summarization. Subagents do run tools, in a fully separate context.

questions.stem: You ask Claude Code to find the 'authentication logic', but the relevant file auth_utils.py never contains that phrase and is not found. Which design decision explains this failure?
options: Its markdown memory files exclude Python sources from search
Its context window is too small to hold the search results
It searches files with Grep pattern matching, not an embedding database, so semantic similarity is missed
Subagent isolation prevents search results from reaching the parent agent
questions.explanation: Claude Code relies on Grep-style pattern search and does not use an embedding database, so 'authentication logic' will not match a file unless the words actually appear in it — semantic similarity is not understood.

questions.stem: The lecture closes with 'LLMs are only as good as the information you give them.' What does this imply for agent builders?
options: Choosing the strongest available model removes the need for memory
The context supplied to the LLM can matter more than the raw strength of the model itself
Agents should minimize context to avoid biasing the model
Memory engineering only matters when using weak models
questions.explanation: Agent memory and context engineering are critical components of good software agents — the lecture states the context given to the LLM may be more important than the strength of the LLM itself, regardless of model choice.
